01_java_main.title: Java Main and Static Entry
01_java_main.description:
A Java program starts from `public static void main(String[] args)`.

```java
public static void main(String[] args)
```

The JVM needs an entry point it can call before any object exists.

q2.question: Why is `main` declared `static`?
options: The JVM must call it before any `Main` instance exists.
Static methods are always faster.
Without `static`, Java cannot print.
explanations: Correct. The entry point must be callable without constructing the class first.
Performance is not the reason.
Printing is unrelated to `static`.
q2.answerReason: The JVM discovers and invokes `main` before your application has created objects.

q3.question: What does `String[] args` represent?
options: A list of loaded classes.
The command-line arguments passed to the program.
The fields declared on the class.
explanations: Loaded classes are unrelated.
Correct. `args` carries process input from the command line.
Fields are unrelated to the method parameter.
q3.answerReason: `args` is just the process argument array provided at startup.

02_kotlin_data_class.title: Kotlin Data Classes
02_kotlin_data_class.description:
Kotlin `data class` generates value-oriented methods from constructor properties.

```kotlin
data class User(val name: String, val age: Int)
```

q2.question: Why use `data class` here?
options: It generates useful value-style methods such as `copy`, `equals`, and `toString`.
It automatically registers a Spring bean.
It makes the object mutable by default.
explanations: Correct. `data class` is a language feature for value-centric types.
Bean registration is separate.
Mutability depends on `val` vs `var`.
q2.answerReason: Data classes are for state-holder types where field-based equality and copying are useful.

q3.question: Which generated feature is especially useful for immutable updates?
options: `hashCode()`
`componentN()`
`copy()`
explanations: `hashCode()` is for hashing, not structural updates.
`componentN()` is for destructuring.
Correct. `copy()` makes small immutable updates ergonomic.
q3.answerReason: `copy()` is one reason Kotlin immutable data modeling feels concise.

03_jvm_memory.title: Stack Frames, References, and Heap Objects
03_jvm_memory.description:
A local reference is not the same thing as the object it points to.

```java
Box b = a;
```

That assignment copies a reference, not the heap object itself.

q2.question: Why does changing `b.value` affect `a.value`?
options: Both variables refer to the same object instance.
Java deep-copies object fields on assignment.
The JVM merges the variable names.
explanations: Correct. Two references point to one object.
Java does not deep-copy objects on assignment.
Compilation does not merge names like that.
q2.answerReason: Assignment of object references copies the reference, not the object.

q3.question: What is actually copied by `Box b = a;`?
options: A new heap object with duplicated fields.
The reference value pointing to the object.
Only the `value` field.
explanations: Simple assignment does not clone the object.
Correct. The object stays one object; the reference is copied.
Java does not copy only one field here.
q3.answerReason: This is why aliasing and mutation through shared references matter on the JVM.

04_spring_di.title: Constructor Injection

q2.question: Why is constructor injection usually preferred?
options: It makes required dependencies explicit and easier to test.
It lets Spring ignore missing dependencies.
It removes the need for interfaces.
explanations: Correct. Constructor injection works well with explicit object design.
Missing required dependencies are still errors.
Interfaces are unrelated.
q2.answerReason: If a class cannot work without a dependency, the constructor is the clearest place to require it.

q3.question: Why does constructor injection fit immutable service design well?
options: It makes fields mutable later.
Dependencies can be required once and stored in fixed fields.
It prevents bean registration.
explanations: Constructor injection usually works with final/immutable dependencies.
Correct. The object starts life fully wired.
Beans are still registered normally.
q3.answerReason: You declare the object’s contract up front instead of hoping fields are injected later.

05_bean_lifecycle.title: Bean Creation and Container Ownership

q2.question: What does it mean for Spring to “manage” a bean?
options: It compiles the class into the JVM.
It removes the need for constructors.
The container controls creation, wiring, and lifecycle of that object.
explanations: Compilation is separate from the container.
Constructors still matter.
Correct. Bean management is about object lifecycle and dependency graph control.
q2.answerReason: Dependency injection works because the container, not arbitrary calling code, controls object construction.

q3.question: Why does container-managed creation matter architecturally?
options: Because wiring can be centralized and cross-cutting lifecycle behavior can be applied consistently.
Because beans no longer need types.
Because it makes persistence automatic for every class.
explanations: Correct. Centralized construction enables consistent configuration and lifecycle control.
Type information still matters.
Persistence is a separate concern.
q3.answerReason: A container becomes useful when object graphs and cross-cutting setup stop being trivial.

06_web_controller.title: Controller to Service Flow
06_web_controller.description:
A controller should stay thin: receive the HTTP request, delegate business logic, and shape the response.

```java
@GetMapping("/users/{id}")
UserResponse get(...)
```

q2.question: What is the better responsibility split?
options: Controller handles transport details; service owns business logic.
Controller should contain all SQL.
Service should parse HTTP annotations directly.
explanations: Correct. Keep transport and domain concerns separated.
Putting SQL in controllers creates tight coupling.
HTTP parsing belongs to the web boundary.
q2.answerReason: Thin controllers scale better because business rules remain reusable and testable.

q3.question: What usually belongs in the controller rather than the service?
options: The core pricing algorithm.
Entity lifecycle mapping.
HTTP-specific concerns like path variables and response status.
explanations: Business rules are usually more reusable in services.
Entity mapping belongs to persistence/domain concerns.
Correct. Controllers are the transport boundary.
q3.answerReason: The controller is where HTTP concepts enter and leave the system.

07_jpa_entity.title: Entity Identity and Persistence Context

q1.question: What is printed before persistence assigns an id?

q2.question: Why is entity identity important?
options: The persistence context tracks one managed instance per database identity.
Entities are just DTOs with annotations.
Identity matters only for native SQL.
explanations: Correct. JPA change tracking and caching depend on identity.
Entities have lifecycle semantics beyond DTOs.
Identity is core to ORM behavior too.
q2.answerReason: If two objects claim one row identity, the persistence context must reason about one managed instance.

q3.question: What changes when an entity becomes managed?
options: It loses its identity field.
JPA begins tracking changes on that instance for flush or commit.
It can no longer be compared by identity.
explanations: Managed entities still have identity.
Correct. Managed means the persistence context observes that object as part of the unit of work.
Identity remains important.
q3.answerReason: Managed entities participate in change tracking because the persistence context now owns that live instance relationship.

08_lazy_loading.title: Lazy Loading and Object Graph Boundaries
08_lazy_loading.description:
ORM relationships are often loaded lazily so the whole graph is not fetched immediately.

```java
member.getOrders()
```

That convenience can hide extra queries if the access pattern is not designed carefully.

q2.question: Why is lazy loading attractive?
options: It eliminates all database queries.
It forces everything into one giant join.
It delays fetching related data until code actually touches it.
explanations: Queries still happen; they are just delayed.
Lazy loading is often used specifically to avoid eager loading everything.
Correct. The idea is to fetch related data on demand.
q2.answerReason: Lazy loading is about delaying work, not eliminating it.

q3.question: Why can lazy loading become a performance problem?
options: Touching related objects in loops can trigger many extra queries unexpectedly.
It prevents entities from having fields.
It disables transactions automatically.
explanations: Correct. The classic risk is hidden N+1 query behavior.
Fields are unaffected.
Transactions are separate.
q3.answerReason: What looks like object navigation in application code may actually be repeated database traffic.

09_transaction_boundary.title: Transaction Boundaries

q2.question: Why use `@Transactional` around related writes?
options: To keep the unit of work atomic if one step fails.
To make methods public automatically.
To avoid JDBC connections.
explanations: Correct. The goal is all-or-nothing state change.
Visibility is unrelated.
Transactions do not remove the need for a database connection.
q2.answerReason: Atomicity matters when several writes should commit or roll back together.

q3.question: What failure does a transaction primarily protect against?
options: Typos in variable names.
The JVM loading classes in the wrong order.
A partial commit where some writes escape before a later step fails.
explanations: Transactions do not solve source-level typing mistakes.
Class loading order is unrelated here.
Correct. Partial persistence is the real business risk.
q3.answerReason: Without a transaction, the system may persist an inconsistent half-completed business action.

10_repository_queries.title: Repository Abstractions and Query Boundaries

q2.question: Why is a repository abstraction useful?
options: It removes the need for a database.
It separates domain-oriented access operations from higher-level business logic.
It turns every query into a transaction automatically.
explanations: A repository still talks to a data source somehow.
Correct. Services can ask for domain data without owning low-level query details.
Transactions are separate.
q2.answerReason: Repositories can keep persistence concerns from leaking through every service method.

q3.question: What is the risk when services build ad hoc queries everywhere?
options: Persistence logic becomes scattered and harder to evolve consistently.
The JVM stops supporting collections.
Controllers no longer compile.
explanations: Correct. Scattered data access creates coupling and inconsistency.
Collections are unaffected.
Compilation is not the main issue.
q3.answerReason: A dedicated data-access boundary helps contain change in how data is fetched and persisted.

11_spring_test.title: Slice Tests and Fast Feedback
11_spring_test.description:
Not every test needs the full application context. Slice tests load only the layer you care about.

```java
@WebMvcTest(UserController.class)
```

q2.question: Why use a slice test such as `@WebMvcTest`?
options: It narrows the loaded context to the web layer for faster, focused tests.
It automatically tests the real transaction log.
It replaces assertions with Spring-generated checks.
explanations: Correct. Focused scope speeds feedback and isolates failures.
A web slice test does not load the whole database stack by default.
You still write assertions yourself.
q2.answerReason: Good test pyramids rely on many fast, narrow tests before full integration checks.

q3.question: Why are focused tests valuable even when you also have integration tests?
options: They replace all need for integration tests.
They isolate one layer and fail faster with a narrower cause.
They guarantee every bean is loaded.
explanations: Broader tests still matter.
Correct. Narrow tests keep failure reasons clearer and feedback faster.
Slice tests intentionally do not load every bean.
q3.answerReason: Fast focused tests catch many issues before you pay the cost of starting the whole application.

12_testcontainers_boundary.title: Integration Tests and Real Infrastructure
12_testcontainers_boundary.description:
Some tests should cross the abstraction boundary and exercise real infrastructure.

```text
controller -> service -> repository -> real database
```

The point is not to replace narrow tests, but to verify behavior at the seams where mocks can lie.

q2.question: Why keep some real integration tests even if slice tests are strong?
options: Because integration tests make unit tests unnecessary.
Because mocks are always wrong.
Because some failures appear only when the real framework, configuration, and infrastructure interact together.
explanations: Integration tests complement rather than eliminate narrow tests.
Mocks are useful, just not sufficient for every risk.
Correct. Real seams can fail in ways isolated tests do not expose.
q2.answerReason: Abstraction is good, but some risks live exactly at the integration boundary.

q3.question: What is the main tradeoff of deeper integration tests?
options: They give broader confidence but are slower and usually less specific when they fail.
They always run faster than pure unit tests.
They remove the need to understand framework configuration.
explanations: Correct. Broader scope means broader signals and higher cost.
Integration tests are usually slower.
They often require even more configuration awareness.
q3.answerReason: Use integration tests deliberately for seam validation, not as the only testing strategy.
